medications.drug_name: Lidocaine (lignocaine) 1% — local infiltration for wound closure
medications.indication_rationale: Local anaesthetic infiltration provides analgesia for irrigation and primary suture closure of the laceration. This is administered IN PERSON at the point of closure — the telehealth AI Doctor arranges the in-person procedure and does NOT administer or prescribe it remotely.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1% lidocaine by local infiltration around the wound, titrated to effect at closure (clinician-administered in person)

medications.indication_rationale: Simple oral analgesia for wound pain; the source safety-net advice references paracetamol as first-line pain relief. Patient-obtainable OTC; adult patient so standard adult dosing applies.

Tetanus prophylaxis would be considered for any wound, but the patient's tetanus immunisation is confirmed up to date, so no booster or immunoglobulin is required. Re-administering would be an error of commission.

Routine prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated for a clean, promptly irrigated and closed traumatic laceration in an immunocompetent patient; they would be over-treatment. Reserve for established infection.

medications.drug_name: Benzodiazepine (e.g. diazepam/lorazepam) for the acute panic
medications.indication_rationale: An acute situational panic reaction is managed with calm reassurance, slow breathing and grounding, and by restoring the environment — not with a sedative, which is not indicated, cannot be prescribed appropriately in this remote acute setting, and risks further disorientation and falls in a visually impaired patient.
contraindications_in_this_case: Fall risk / disorientation would be worsened by sedation
No indication for pharmacological anxiolysis in an acute situational panic reaction

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Urgent home safety and orientation-and-mobility assessment (e.g. Vision Australia) to restore a safe, predictable layout and reduce recurrent-fall risk after the disruption.
Wound review, dressing support and suture removal, especially important as the patient cannot visually self-monitor the wound.
If panic/anxiety about mobility recurs or persists, brief psychological support for coping and confidence with mobility may help.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Coach a simple in-the-moment grounding and slow-breathing routine to terminate the acute panic, with the spouse providing calm verbal orientation.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Situational panic settles with reassurance and paced breathing as environmental control is restored.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: After visitors leave, systematically trace the edges of each room with the cane to reset the internal map before moving freely.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Re-mapping restores accurate spatial awareness and independent mobility after any layout change.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Enforce a strict household 'no silent moving' rule — anyone who moves an object states it aloud and returns furniture to its usual place.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Verbal confirmation of any change keeps the patient's environmental map accurate and prevents trips.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Address the recent circadian/sleep disruption to reduce fatigue-related loss of spatial awareness.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Fatigue and altered circadian rhythm contributed to reduced awareness on the day of the fall.

patient_education_points.point: This is a deep, gaping cut that needs to be professionally cleaned and closed today — keep firm pressure on it and get seen in person; it is not something to just bandage and leave.
Keep firm, continuous pressure with a clean cloth and raise the leg to slow the bleeding until you are seen; if it soaks through or won't stop, call 000.
Because you can't see the wound yourself, have your spouse check the dressing and the skin around it twice a day for spreading redness, heat, swelling or discharge.
Watch for infection over the next days — increasing pain, spreading redness or warmth, pus, or fever — and seek care promptly if any appear.
Keep the dressing clean and dry; take simple pain relief such as paracetamol as needed.
Have furniture returned to its usual positions and re-map the room with your cane before moving around freely again to prevent another fall.

follow_up_plan.interval: in-person wound closure today; GP review in ~7 days for wound check and suture removal
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: wound healing and signs of infection, suture removal, resolution of the panic/anxiety, and completion of the home-safety/orientation assessment
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: bleeding that will not stop with pressure, feeling faint or unwell, increasing pain, spreading redness/heat/pus or fever, or any further fall

must_include_items: Recognise the gaping laceration needs urgent SAME-DAY in-person professional cleaning and closure (telehealth cannot suture)
Coach the carer to control the bleeding with firm continuous direct pressure and leg elevation
De-escalate the acute panic with calm reassurance, slow breathing and grounding, using the spouse present
Confirm tetanus status (already up to date — no booster needed) and screen for fracture, head injury and a syncopal cause of the fall
Address the recurrent-fall hazard: restore the home layout and arrange an urgent home-safety / orientation assessment
Give clear wound-infection safety-netting with carer inspection, since the patient cannot self-monitor visually
acceptable_alternatives: Same-day GP, urgent care clinic, or a nurse-led wound service are all acceptable venues for the in-person closure
If bleeding cannot be controlled with pressure, escalation to ED / 000 is the correct alternative
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange urgent in-person wound closure (advising the patient to just dress it at home)
Failing to give direct-pressure bleeding-control advice
Attributing everything to anxiety and omitting the fracture / head-injury / syncope screen (diagnostic overshadowing)
Failing to address the environmental fall hazard and recurrent-fall risk
errors_of_commission: Attempting to talk the patient or carer through closing/suturing the wound at home
Prescribing routine prophylactic antibiotics for a clean laceration
Recommending a benzodiazepine or other sedative for the situational panic
Over-triaging a controllable venous ooze to an emergency ambulance (000) / ED
Re-administering tetanus when it is already up to date
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising the need for urgent same-day in-person wound closure AND giving bleeding-control advice AND de-escalating the panic. Over-triaging to 000/ED for a controllable venous ooze, or under-triaging to home self-care, are both scored failures; dismissing the presentation as 'just a panic attack' without the injury/fall screen is a critical reasoning error.
